elements.sent-2411-001: Recently in robotics, Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a transformative approach, enabling robots to execute complex tasks by integrating visual and linguistic inputs within an end-to-end learning framework.
elements.sent-2411-002: While VLA models offer significant capabilities, they also introduce new attack surfaces, making them vulnerable to adversarial attacks.
elements.sent-2411-003: With these vulnerabilities largely unexplored, this paper systematically quantifies the robustness of VLA-based robotic systems.
elements.sent-2411-004: Recognizing the unique demands of robotic execution, our attack objectives target the inherent spatial and functional characteristics of robotic systems.
elements.sent-2411-005: In particular, we introduce an untargeted position-aware attack objective that leverages spatial foundations to destabilize robotic actions, and a targeted attack objective that manipulates the robotic trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-006: Additionally, we design an adversarial patch generation approach that places a small, colorful patch within the camera's view, effectively executing the attack in both digital and physical environments.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our evaluation reveals a marked degradation in task success rates, with up to a 100\% reduction across a suite of simulated robotic tasks, highlighting critical security gaps in current VLA architectures.
elements.sent-2411-008: By unveiling these vulnerabilities and proposing actionable evaluation metrics, this work advances both the understanding and enhancement of safety for VLA-based robotic systems, underscoring the necessity for developing robust defense strategies prior to physical-world deployments.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.38\textwidth]{figs/radar.pdf} \caption{Fatal failure rates resulting from different attacking schemes~(\ie, UADA, UPA, and TMA) underscore vulnerabilities in VLA-based robotic systems.} \label{fig:problems}
elements.sent-2411-009: In the movie Finch, set in a post-apocalyptic future, an intelligent robot navigates complex themes, underscoring the importance of interactive safety with its human master.
elements.sent-2411-010: Once speculative, this portrayal is now increasingly plausible with the rise of visually-conditioned language models (VLMs)~\cite{wang2024mmpt, xu2024vision capable of seamlessly interpreting both visual and linguistic contexts.}
elements.sent-2411-011: A notable realization of this potential can be seen in Vision-Language Action (VLA) models~\cite{wang2024towards, ding2025quar, kim24openvla, wen2024tinyvla , which integrate VLMs into robotic systems to enable end-to-end learning that encompasses both high-level planning and the generation of low-level robot control actions.}
elements.sent-2411-012: While VLA models demonstrate a promising step toward generalist robotic intelligence, they also introduce potential vulnerabilities that remain underexplored.
elements.sent-2411-013: This work addresses the pressing need to investigate the new attack surfaces associated with VLA-based robotic systems.
elements.sent-2411-014: To gain deeper insights into these vulnerabilities, we analyze VLA-based models and general robotic operations, highlighting two key characteristics crucial for designing effective adversarial attacks.
elements.sent-2411-015: First, creating attack objectives for robotic systems requires consideration of the physical dynamics and constraints intrinsic to robotic movement.
elements.sent-2411-016: Conventional adversarial attacks, in this case, often fail to produce significant deviations in intended actions because they disregard these constraints.
elements.sent-2411-017: Second, VLA models generate control signals that function as a time series of K-class predictions (see \S\ref{preliminary ), making it essential to design attacks that exploit the temporal dependencies within these sequences to cause substantial disruptions in robotic behavior.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Achieving attacks that are effective in both digital simulations and real-world environments remains critical yet challenging.
elements.sent-2411-019: To address these challenges, our work intensifies the adversarial threats posed to VLA-based systems by developing specialized attack objectives and designing effective attack methods.
elements.sent-2411-020: Specifically, we formulate an Action Discrepancy Objective aimed at maximizing the normalized action discrepancy within VLA-based robotic systems.
elements.sent-2411-021: This strategy ensures that, at each decision point, the robot's behavior can diverge from the optimal trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-022: Additionally, we introduce Geometry-Aware Objective that considers the robot's movement in three-dimensional space, characterized by three degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-023: By optimizing the cosine similarity between adversarial and ground-truth directions, we induce deviations in the robot's movement direction from its intended path, increasing the likelihood of task failure.
elements.sent-2411-024: To achieve these attack objectives, we develop a straightforward yet effective Patch-Based Attack targeting VLA-based robotic systems.
elements.sent-2411-025: This approach enables adversarial attacks in both digital and physical settings, revealing substantial vulnerabilities within the VLA-based system.
elements.sent-2411-026: Together, these innovations yield several significant contributions: \ding{182 This work presents a pioneering and comprehensive analysis of vulnerabilities within VLA-based robotic systems, a new paradigm for training generalized robotic policies using generative foundation models.}
elements.sent-2411-027: We reveal critical threats to adversarial attacks~(see Fig.~\ref{fig:problems ), emphasizing the urgent need to enhance robustness before real-world deployment.}
elements.sent-2411-028: This offers valuable insights for the research community to explore systemic failures in similar concurrent generative foundation models.
elements.sent-2411-029: This highlights the effectiveness of our attack strategies~(see \S\ref{subsec:main_results ).}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figs/mainfig.pdf} \caption{Overall Adversarial Framework. The robot captures an input image, processes it through a vision-language model to generate tokens representing actions, and then uses an action de-tokenizer for discrete bin prediction. The model is optimized with adversarial objectives focusing on various discrepancies and geometries (\ie, UADA, UPA, TMA). Forward propagation is shown in black, and backpropagation is highlighted in \textcolor[HTML]{E26C9F}{pink}. These objectives aim to maximize errors and minimize task performance, with visual emphasis on 3D-space manipulation and a focus on generating adversarial perturbation $\delta$ during task execution, such as picking up a can.} \label{fig:main}
elements.sent-2411-030: Developing generalist robots~\cite{Nilsson1973, BillardscienceTrends, Kroemer2021ReviewRobotLearning, Ingrand2017ReviewDeliberation, Cui2021ScienceRobotic requires models not only handle varied interactions but also maintain robustness in unstructured environments.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Early intelligent robots~\cite{Murray1994mathematical, Mason2001Mechanics, Mason2018Toward, moudgal1994rule, zhu2021rule relied on rule-based approaches, effective only in controlled settings and requiring extensive reprogramming for new tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-032: The advent of deep learning~\cite{Levine2018CNNHandEye, Pinto2016Supersizing, Finn2017MetaLearning, JamesBD18TaskEmbedded,jin-etal-2024-impact and reinforcement learning~\cite{tang2024deep, wang2024research, al2024reinforcement has shifted this paradigm to adapt through data, increasing robot versatility and reducing manual reconfiguration.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Recent studies~\cite{Brohan2023rt1, MeesHB2022What, Pong2020Skew have further advanced the development and potential of large-scale visually-conditioned language models (VLMs) as key enablers for generalist robots, demonstrating promising generalization across a variety of scenes~\cite{Brianna2023RT2, Jiang2022VIMA, Octo2024Octo, huang2023embodied, li2023vision, cui2024collaborative .}}
elements.sent-2411-034: One of the notable examples is OpenVLA~\cite{kim24openvla , which integrates dual visual encoders with a pre-trained large language model to enable observation-to-action capabilities through instruction-following~\cite{liu2024improved, liu2024visual, karamcheti2024prismatic, chen2023pali, zhao2023svit, wang2024mmpt .}}
elements.sent-2411-035: By aligning visual and textual semantics, OpenVLA facilitates complex scenario understanding and end-to-end action generation, showing impressive generalization when executing previously unseen task instructions.
elements.sent-2411-036: Despite its success in generalist robotic applications, the vulnerability of such systems remains largely unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-037: This study aims to address this gap by investigating adversarial threats to VLA-based robotic systems.
elements.sent-2411-038: Current human-robot interaction risks include physical discomfort or injury~\cite{Guiochet2017Safety, benos2020safety, berx2022identification .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Considering these risks, ensuring safety during interactions becomes the top priority as many literature outlined~\cite{villani2018survey,halme2018review, lasota2017survey .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Existing robotic safety strategies have primarily concentrated on mitigating naturally occurring hazards~\cite{lasota2017survey .}
elements.sent-2411-041: These strategies can be generally categorized into pre-collision or post-collision interventions based on the timing of manage hazards~\cite{Lasota2017SafeHRI .}
elements.sent-2411-042: Pre-collision approaches aim to prevent severe hazards by implementing physical constraints, such as controlling force and speed~\cite{iso_10218,iso_15066 , defining safety zones~\cite{Vogel2013safezone and predicting human actions to avoid potentially dangerous robot movements~\cite{Koppula2016CRF,Koppula2013CRF,Mainprice2013GMM .}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Post-collision strategies, on the other hand, focus on detection and response after a harmful action has occurred~\cite{Luca2006Collision,Golz2015SVM .}
elements.sent-2411-044: While there is substantial research on robotic safety, the integration of AI into robotic policies poses new challenges, where attackers can manipulate AI-driven robot and execute malicious actions.
elements.sent-2411-045: Such threats introduce additional layers of risk and complexity.
elements.sent-2411-046: In this paper, we primarily focus on adversarial threats.
elements.sent-2411-047: Adversarial attacks serve as critical tools for assessing model vulnerabilities, particularly in robotics, where models operate in dynamic, real-world settings.
elements.sent-2411-048: Traditional gradient-based, pixel-level attacks~\cite{Goodfellow2015FGSM,Madry2018twords,Du2022Physical,Wang2019advPattern,Liu2019Perceptualpatch,Su2019OnePixel exploit model gradients to calculate malicious perturbations, achieving high attack success rates in digital environments.}
elements.sent-2411-049: For physical-world attacks, patch-based methods~\cite{brown2018adversarialpatch, Sharif2016advglass, Xu2020AdvTShirt, AthalyeEOT2018, wu2024adversarial offer a practical alternative for real-world applications by introducing physically realizable perturbations that retain efficacy under varied conditions, such as angle or lighting changes, making them highly applicable for robotic systems.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Therefore, considering VLA-based models enable robot execution in real-world scenarios, this work employs a patch-based attack strategy.
elements.sent-2411-051: VLA models~\cite{Octo2024Octo,Huang2023VoxPoser, chiang2024mobility couple visual and linguistic modalities for perception-action alignment.}
elements.sent-2411-052: The continuous, high-dimensional nature of visual inputs~\cite{Szegedy2014Intriguing,Madry2018twords, QiHP0WM24Visual makes them the most likely target for adversarial perturbations, as attackers can subtly alter visual data in ways that are difficult to detect~\cite{Carlini017Bypassing,AthalyeC018Obfuscated,Tramer22Detecting .}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Consequently, this work applies attacks on the visual modality to achieve robust manipulations across diverse environments.
elements.sent-2411-054: To the best of our knowledge, this study is one of the earliest attempts to formally define adversarial objectives for VLA models, targeting gaps in adversarial robustness through the model’s geometric constraints (\eg, spatial dependencies in visual input) and architectural nuances (\eg, cross-modal information integration).
elements.sent-2411-055: This section outlines our proposed methodology.
elements.sent-2411-056: We first review the core algorithmic principles of VLA in \S\ref{preliminary , which serves as the foundation for the adversarial scheme designs.}
elements.sent-2411-057: We then present two types of untargeted adversarial attacks, including Untargeted Action Discrepancy Attack~(UADA) in \S\ref{Normalized_Action_Discrepancy_Attack and Untargeted Position-aware Attack~(UPA) in \S\ref{Geometry_aware_Attack .}}
elements.sent-2411-058: In \S\ref{Target_Attack , we further include Targeted Manipulation Attack~(TMA), designed to direct the robot toward specific erroneous execution.}
elements.sent-2411-059: The implementation details are presented in \S\ref{Attack_Pipeline .}
elements.sent-2411-060: The overall framework is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:main .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Vision-Language Action models~\cite{Brianna2023RT2,kim24openvla (see Fig.~\ref{fig:main ) are built on large language models integrated with visual encoders, enabling robots to interpret human instructions and process visual input from a camera to perform context-aware actions.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: VLA models~\cite{kim24openvla,Brianna2023RT2,Li2024LLaRA generally abstract continuous action prediction into a classification problem by discretizing robot actions.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Specifically, they first discretize the continuous probabilities into class labels $c = \arg \max F(x)$.
elements.sent-2411-064: An action de-tokenizer then generates actions $y=DT(c)$.
elements.sent-2411-065: By categorizing action values into discrete class labels, the model converts continuous probability outputs into discrete signals, this simplification facilitates quicker convergence and faster training times, and is commonly used for VLA-based models~\cite{kim24openvla,Brianna2023RT2,Li2024LLaRA .}
elements.sent-2411-066: In this work, we focus on a 7-degree-of-freedom (DoF) robotic arm~\cite{haddadin2022franka .}
elements.sent-2411-067: At each step, an action consists of 7DoFs with specific physical significance in three-dimensional Cartesian space, represented by: \begin{equation} A=[\Delta{P_x}, \Delta{P_y}, \Delta{P_z}, \Delta{R_x}, \Delta{R_y}, \Delta{R_z}, \text{gripper}],\end{equation} where $\Delta{P_{x,y,z }$ and $\Delta{R_{x,y,z }$ denote relative positional and rotational changes along the x-, y-, and z-axis, discretized into 256 uniform bins across the action range~\cite{kim24openvla .}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: The $gripper$ state is binary, indicating its open or closed state.
elements.sent-2411-069: This control design presents a unique challenge for adversarial attacks, as finely divided bins result in minimal action discrepancies between neighboring bins (e.g., $\pm$0.007/bin).
elements.sent-2411-070: This means that even if an attack shifts the classification to an adjacent bin, the resulting action discrepancy has minimal impact real-world performance.
elements.sent-2411-071: We first focus on maximizing action discrepancies by introducing Untargeted Action Discrepancy Attack (UADA).
elements.sent-2411-072: This attack is based on the observation that larger robot actions usually correlate with intense physical movements, which, in turn, may amplify the potential for real-world hazards~\cite{iso_10218,iso_15066,ISO13482 .}
elements.sent-2411-073: Specifically to UADA, the attack target is one or combination of 7DoFs, defind as $y^I=\{y^{i |i\in[1,\dots,7]\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-074: To define UADA’s objective, we first determine the maximum action discrepancy $d_{\text{max }$, which represents the largest deviation that can occur within the allowed range for each DoF.}
elements.sent-2411-075: This is calculated by measuring the distance between the ground truth $y$ and its action range $[y_{\min ,y_{\max ]$ as: \begin{equation} d_{max}^i (y) = \max(| y^i - y^i_{\min} |, |y^i - y^i_{\max} |).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-076: We next calculate the applied action discrepancy $d_{\text{applied ^i(x,y)$ $=|F(x)^i-y^i|$ in order to measure the deviation between the model’s output and the ground truth, where $F(x)^i$ is the $i$-th action output of model $F(x)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: In this sense, we can evaluate the Normalized Action Discrepancy (NAD) as the drifting ratio between $d_{applied ^i$ and $d_{max ^i$ as: \begin{equation}\rm{NAD} = \frac{1}{I}\sum_i^I d^i_{\text{applied}}/d^i_{\text{max}}. \label{formula:AD}\end{equation} Since VLA models are currently operated via predicting discrete token labels (see \S\ref{preliminary ), we then need to bridge action discrepancy derived from these discrete labels into learnable targets.}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Specifically, we weighted sum the model output probabilities across $J$ bins by their own class label $j$ as: \begin{equation}d_{\text{applied}}^{i \prime}(x,y)=| \sum_{j=1}^{J}(j\cdot\mathcal{F}(x)_j^i) - c^i |,\end{equation} where $\mathcal{F (\cdot)_j^i$ is the model output probability of $i$-th DoF's $j$-th bin.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Finally, the attack objective for UADA is defined as minimizing the ratio of weighted sum $d^{\prime _{\text{applied }$ to $d_{max (y^i)$: \begin{equation} \mathcal{L}_{\text{UADA}} = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim\mathcal{X}}\left[\sum^I_i\frac{d^{i \prime}_{\text{applied}}(x+\delta,y)}{d^i_{\max}(y)}\right]^{-1},\end{equation} where $x,y$ is a benign image and action pair, sampled from distribution $\mathcal{X}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: UADA defines the objective functions tailored for the robot’s action space, allowing adversarial patches to create significant, lasting disruptions in task performance.
elements.sent-2411-081: In this design, we uniquely leverage the model’s discrete action structure to design attacks that amplify meaningful discrepancies in robot behavior.
elements.sent-2411-082: We further extend untargeted adversarial attacks to consider the positional dynamics within VLA models.
elements.sent-2411-083: In typical task execution, precise and directed movements of the end-effector towards designated goals are essential, with actions mapped in three-dimensional space.
elements.sent-2411-084: The positional degrees of freedom, $y^{pos = [y^1, y^2, y^3]$ encapsulates the directional movements within 3D for effective robotic control.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Recognizing the importance of $y^{pos}$ in controlling the end-effector's path, we introduce a position-aware attack to disrupt the intended movement trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-086: This type of adversarial vulnerability remains largely unexplored but has significant implications for task failure: the attack objective can steer the end-effector away from its intended path by introducing directional perturbations, amplifying errors and causing substantial trajectory distortions.
elements.sent-2411-087: Formally,$_{\!}$we define the Untargeted Position-aware Attack~(UPA) objective as: \begin{equation} \mathcal{L}_\text{UPA}= \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim\mathcal{X}}\big[\alpha\frac{y^{pos}_{adv} \cdot y^{pos}}{\|y^{pos}_{adv}\| \|y^{pos}\|}+\beta\frac{1}{||y^{pos}_{adv}-y^{pos}||_2}\big], \label{eq:gaattack}\end{equation} where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the hyperparameters that balance between the directionality and magnitude of the perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-088: By integrating geometric awareness into our attack, this approach generates perturbations that induce cumulative deviations in the robot's trajectory, even with minimal adjustments.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Adversarial Patch Attack.} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE { Input:} $\mathcal{X}$: dataset; $\delta$: adversarial patch; $\mathcal{L}_o$: objective function; $\mathcal{F}$: vision language action model; $\mathcal{T}(\cdot)$: Transfromation; $\phi,\psi$ are two hyperparameters for transformation; T: maximum iteration steps; $k$: maximum inner-loop steps. \STATE { Initialize:} $\delta \sim \mathcal{U}[0,1)$, $\mathcal{L}_o \in \{\mathcal{L}_{\text{UADA}}, \mathcal{L}_{\text{UPA}}, \mathcal{L}_{\text{TMA}}\} $ \FOR{$i = 1, 2, \cdots, T$} \STATE $(x,y) \sim \mathcal{X}$ \FOR{$i = 1, 2, \cdots, k$} \label{alg:innerloop} \STATE $shx,shy \sim \mathcal{U}(-\phi,\phi)$, $\theta \sim \mathcal{U}(-\psi,\psi)$ \STATE $y_{\text{pred}} \leftarrow \mathcal{F}(\mathcal{T}(x+\delta))$ \COMMENT{$\rhd$ \blue{Feedforward} } \label{alg:eot} \STATE $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_o}{\partial \delta} \leftarrow \mathcal{L}_o(y_{\text{pred}},\cdot)$ \COMMENT{$\rhd$ \blue{Backpropagation} } \STATE $\delta \leftarrow \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_o}{\partial \delta}$\COMMENT{$\rhd$ \blue{Update} } \STATE Clip $\delta$ into normal pixel range. \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic} \label{algorithm:1}

elements.sent-2411-089: In addition to the aforementioned untargeted attacks, we also explore the adversarial vulnerabilities of VLA models with a targeted strategy.
elements.sent-2411-090: This attack aims to mislead the models into predicting specific trajectories, resulting in precise alterations.
elements.sent-2411-091: These targeted alterations can potentially drive malicious behaviors or induce task failure.
elements.sent-2411-092: The objective of the targeted manipulation attack is: \begin{equation} \mathcal{L_\text{TMA}}=\mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim\mathcal{X}}[\mathcal{H}(\mathcal{F}(x + \delta), y^I_T)],\end{equation} where $ y^I_T=\{y^{i =t|i\in[1,\dots,7],t\in\mathcal{R}\}$ represents the adversarial target; and $\mathcal{H (\cdot,\cdot)$ denotes the Cross-Entropy loss, which measures the error between the predicted state under adversarial perturbation and the desired target state.}}
elements.sent-2411-093: By steering the robot towards an adversarial target across successive time steps, our approach manipulates the trajectory and undermines task performance, ultimately leading to a substantial disruption of intended operations.
elements.sent-2411-094: Furthermore, manipulating any individual DoF or a combination thereof can also severely compromise task success, given that the errors in one dimension tend to propagate and magnify over time during execution (see Fig.~\ref{fig:qualitative ).}
elements.sent-2411-095: The implementation of our pipeline is detailed in Algorithm~\ref{algorithm:1 .}

elements.sent-2411-098: To improve the robustness of our attack under real-world scenarios, we employ random geometric transformations $\mathcal{T (\cdot)$ in line \ref{alg:eot with affine and rotation transformations~\cite{AthalyeEOT2018 as: \begin{equation} \mathcal{T}(\cdot)= \begin{bmatrix} 1 sh_y sh_x 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} \cos \theta -\sin \theta \sin \theta \cos \theta \end{bmatrix}, \label{eq:trans}\end{equation} where $sh_x, sh_y \sim \mathcal{U (-\phi, \phi)$ are shear factors, and $\theta \sim \mathcal{U (-\psi, \psi)$ denotes the rotation angle, both sampled from uniform distributions during inner loop optimization step $k$.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-102: We conduct simulation attacks on BridgeData V2~\cite{bridgev2 and LIBERO~\cite{LIBREO .}}
elements.sent-2411-103: BridgeData V2~\cite{bridgev2 is a real-world dataset comprising $24$ diverse environments and $13$ distinct skills, such as grasping, placing, and object rearrangement, with a total of $60,096$ trajectories.}
elements.sent-2411-104: LIBERO~\cite{LIBREO is a simulation dataset designed to evaluate models across four distinct task types~(Spatial, Object, Goal, Long), each containing $10$ tasks with $50$ human-teleoperated demonstrations.}
elements.sent-2411-105: Notably, LIBERO-Long combines diverse objects, layouts, and long-horizon tasks, making them challenging for complex, multi-step planning, thus we choose LIBERO-Long to conduct UADA, UPA and TMA.. Robot Setups.

elements.sent-2411-107: The sensing system includes one RGB webcam in the fixed position with a shoulder view.. Victim VLA Models.

elements.sent-2411-112: The second uses a model trained on physical world data from the BridgeData v2 dataset~\cite{bridgev2 with the openvla-7B model~\cite{kim24openvla .}}
elements.sent-2411-113: This approach allows us to assess our methods on both simulated and real-world data.
elements.sent-2411-114: Subsequently, we evaluate the performance of generated adversarial patches on victim models~(\ie, OpenVLA LIBERO variants) trained on different tasks to rigorously prove the robustness and effectiveness of our method.

elements.sent-2411-179: A particularly noteworthy observation is that some of these patches bear a striking resemblance to the structural joints of a robotic arm.
elements.sent-2411-180: Given the strong similarity between these patches and robotic arms in appearance, we hypothesize that these VLA-based models, in their current training paradigms, are often limited to a single robotic system operating within restricted camera views.
elements.sent-2411-181: This constrained perspective/camera pose may inadvertently induce a learned representation bias, where adversarial perturbations align with prominent structural features of the training robot's appearance in order to deceive current victim models.
elements.sent-2411-182: Given that these patches are model-specific and reflect the spatial and visual patterns of the training environment, we have a reasonable concern that they may compromise VLA-based models' generalizability and robustness in physical-world deployments.. elements.sent-2411-183: Our work highlights the pressing need for a paradigm shift in the VLA-based models' training strategies in order to successfully defend our attacks.

elements.sent-2411-185: Another possible solution is to leverage the logical relationships within the robot arm’s physical structure to estimate the actual position of each joint.
elements.sent-2411-186: By predicting joint positions deviated from physical constraints, we can eliminate unreasonable adversarial samples, even if they are similar to actual joint(s).
elements.sent-2411-187: While VLA models have gained significant popularity due to their substantial robot capabilities, this study, for the first time, establishes specific robotic attack objectives and demonstrates that these models are in fact vulnerable to various types of attacks.

elements.sent-2411-246: JPEG compression applies compression algorithms to the input images prior to feeding them into the depth estimation network, aiming to disrupt adversarial patterns.
elements.sent-2411-247: We test compression quality levels ranging from 50 to 10, with lower quality levels corresponding to higher compression rates.
elements.sent-2411-248: Gaussian noise introduces zero-mean Gaussian noise to the input image, leveraging its randomness to counteract the structured nature of adversarial perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-249: The standard deviation of the noise varies from 0.01 to 0.1, with higher values introducing stronger noise.
elements.sent-2411-250: Median blur smooths the image by replacing each pixel value with the median of its surrounding pixels, using square kernel sizes from 3 to 9; larger kernel sizes produce stronger smoothing effects.
elements.sent-2411-251: Bit-depth reduction remaps the standard 8-bit depth of RGB channels to smaller bit depths, reducing the color space and potentially disrupting adversarial perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-252: We evaluate cases with bit depths ranging from 6 bits to 3 bits.
elements.sent-2411-253: These defense techniques are evaluated to understand the robustness of our attack~(results see in Fig.~\ref{fig:patchsize ).}

elements.sent-2411-261: Future research on attacks against VLA-based models can potentially be conducted on two key objectives: enhancing camouflage and ensuring practical feasibility.
elements.sent-2411-262: Improving camouflage involves reducing detection probability by generating adversarial patches that seamlessly integrate with the environment, leveraging natural patterns and context-aware design.
elements.sent-2411-263: Furthermore, future efforts should avoid targeting redundant DoF and instead concentrate on critical components that are most likely to disrupt task performance.
elements.sent-2411-264: This can be achieved by leveraging task-specific knowledge and employing advanced optimization techniques to maximize the effectiveness of such attacks while aligning with real-world physical constraints.
elements.sent-2411-265: In particular, we introduce two untargeted attack objectives that leverage spatial foundations to destabilize robotic actions, and a targeted attack objective that manipulates the robotic trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-266: By unveiling these vulnerabilities and proposing actionable evaluation metrics, we advance both the understanding and enhancement of safety for VLA-based robotic systems, underscoring the necessity for continuously developing robust defense strategies prior to physical-world deployments\footnote{Our code is available at \url{https://github.com/William-wAng618/roboticAttack .}}

elements.sent-2411-270: While VLA models demonstrate a promising step toward generalist robotic intelligence, they also introduce potential vulnerabilities that remain largely underexplored.
elements.sent-2411-271: This work, in light of this view, pioneers the understandings of the pressing need to investigate the new attack surfaces associated with VLA-based robotic systems.
elements.sent-2411-272: To address these challenges, our work intensifies the adversarial threats posed to VLA-based systems by both developing specialized attack objectives and designing effective attack methods.
elements.sent-2411-273: Specifically, we formulate an Action Discrepancy Objective aimed at maximizing the action discrepancy within VLA-based robotic systems.
elements.sent-2411-274: To achieve these attack objectives, we develop straightforward yet effective Patch-Based Attacks targeting VLA-based robotic systems.
elements.sent-2411-275: Altogether, these innovations yield several significant contributions: \ding{182 This work presents a pioneering and comprehensive analysis of vulnerabilities within VLA-based robotic systems, a new paradigm for training generalized robotic policies using generative foundation models.}

elements.sent-2411-288: At each step, an action consists of 7 DoFs with specific physical significance in three-dimensional Cartesian space, represented by: \begin{equation} A=[\Delta{P_x}, \Delta{P_y}, \Delta{P_z}, \Delta{R_x}, \Delta{R_y}, \Delta{R_z}, \text{gripper}],\end{equation} where $\Delta{P_{x,y,z }$ and $\Delta{R_{x,y,z }$ denote relative positional and rotational changes along the x-, y-, and z-axis, discretized into 256 uniform bins across each DoFs' action bounds $[y_{\min ^i,y_{\max ^i]$~\cite{kim24openvla .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-289: This means that even if an attack shifts the classification to an adjacent bin, the resulting action discrepancy has minimal impact on real-world performance.
elements.sent-2411-290: To exacerbate action discrepancies, we introduce the Untargeted Action Discrepancy Attack (UADA), which aims to maximize deviations in robot actions.
elements.sent-2411-291: For UADA, the attack target is one or a combination of 7 DoFs, defined as $y^I_{gt =\{y^{i _{gt |i\in[1,\dots,7]\}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-292: To define UADA's objective, we first identify the most distant action $y_{adv ^i$, which maximizes the discrepancy from the $i$-th DoF ground truth action $y^i$.}
elements.sent-2411-293: This action, for each DoF, corresponds to its own action bound $[y_{\min ^i,y_{\max ^i]$ as: \begin{equation}y_{adv}^i = \begin{cases} y_{\max}^i, \text{if } |y_{\max}^i-y^i_{gt}| \geq |y_{\min}^i-y^i_{gt}| y_{\min}^i, \text{otherwise} \end{cases}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-294: Instead of directly using $y_{adv ^i$ as the misclassification target, we introduce a soft attack objective to capture the discrepancy between actions, ensuring smooth gradient optimization and stable attack performance.}
elements.sent-2411-295: Specifically, due to the physical action magnitude information contains in bin labels, we reweight the output probability $F(x)^i_{bins}\in\mathcal{R ^{_{1\times{bins }$ using normalized bin labels $y^i_{bins =[\frac{1 {bins ,\dots,1]$, where $\lfloor y^i_{bins \rfloor$ and $\lceil y^i_{bins \rceil$ correspond to the normalized values of $y_{\min ^i$ and $y_{\max ^i$, respectively.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-296: The reweighting process is defined as: \begin{equation}y_{soft}^{i}= \sum_{bins=1}^{n} F(x+\delta)^i_{bins} \otimes y^i_{bins},\end{equation} where $\otimes$ denotes Hadamard Product, $\delta$ is the adversarial patch and $y_{soft ^{i}$ represents the $i$-th DoF's soft action.}
elements.sent-2411-297: Finally, the objective of UADA is to minimize the discrepancy between the soft and the most distant actions, which is: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\text{UADA}} = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim\mathcal{X}}\sum_{i}^{I} (y_{soft}^{i}-y_{adv}^i)^2,\end{equation} UADA defines the objective function $\mathcal{L _{\text{UADA }$ tailored for attacking the robot’s action space, allowing adversarial patches to create significant, lasting disruptions in task performance.}}

elements.sent-2411-299: The positional DoFs, $A^p = [\Delta{P_x , \Delta{P_y , \Delta{P_z ]$ encapsulates the directional movements within 3D for effective robotic control.}}}
elements.sent-2411-300: Recognizing the importance of $A^p=DT(y^{p )$ in controlling the end-effector's path, we introduce a position-aware attack to disrupt the intended movement trajectory.}
elements.sent-2411-301: Formally,$_{\!}$we define the Untargeted Position-aware Attack~(UPA) objective as: \begin{equation} \mathcal{L}_\text{UPA}= \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim\mathcal{X}}\big[\alpha\frac{y^{p}_{adv} \cdot y^{p}}{\|y^{p}_{adv}\| \|y^{p}\|}+\beta\frac{1}{||y^{p}_{adv}-y^{p}||_2}\big], \label{eq:gaattack}\end{equation} where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the hyperparameters that balance between the directionality and magnitude of the perturbations.

elements.subsection-2411-017: A Novel Evaluation Metric: Normalized Action Discrepancy
elements.sent-2411-306: To assess the potential physical impact of adversarial patches, we introduce Normalized Action Discrepancy~(NAD) as a fine-grained metric for evaluating discrepancies at the action level.
elements.sent-2411-307: NAD serves as a complementary measurement alongside the coarse-grained Failure Rate (FR) at the task level, providing a detailed analysis of deviations during action execution.
elements.sent-2411-308: To define the NAD, we first determine the maximum action discrepancy $d^i_{\text{max }$, which represents the largest deviation that can occur within the allowed range for $i$-th DoF.}
elements.sent-2411-309: This is computed by measuring the L1 distance between the ground truth $y^i_{gt}$ and its action bound $[y^i_{\min ,y^i_{\max ]$ as: \begin{equation} d_{\text{max}}^i (y) = \max \big[DT(| y^i_{gt} - y^i_{\min} |), DT(|y^i_{gt} - y^i_{\max} |)\big].\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-310: We next calculate the applied action discrepancy $d_{\text{applied ^i(x,y)$ $=|DT(F(x)^i)-DT(y^i_{gt )|$ to measure the deviation between the model’s output and ground truth.}}}
elements.sent-2411-311: We define the NAD as the drifting ratio between $d_{\text{applied ^i$ and $d_{\text{max ^i$ as: \begin{equation}\rm{NAD} = \frac{1}{I}\sum_i^I \frac{d^i_{\text{applied}}}{d^i_{\text{max}}}, \label{formula:AD}\end{equation} where $I\in[1,\dots,7]$ represents a single or a combination of DoF(s).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-312: In this way, NAD provides normalized measurement of action deviations, allowing for a consistent evaluation of adversarial impact across varying DoF(s).

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Adversarial Patch Attack.} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE { Input:} $\mathcal{X}$: dataset; $\delta$: patch; $\mathcal{L}_o$: attack objective; $\mathcal{F}$: VLA model; $\mathcal{T}(\cdot)$: transformations; $\phi,\psi$: transforma -tion params; T: attack steps; $k$: inner-loop steps. \STATE { Initialize:} $\delta \sim \mathcal{U}[0,1)$, $\mathcal{L}_o \in \{\mathcal{L}_{\text{UADA}}, \mathcal{L}_{\text{UPA}}, \mathcal{L}_{\text{TMA}}\} $ \FOR{$i = 1, 2, \cdots, T$} \FOR{$i = 1, 2, \cdots, k$} \label{alg:innerloop} \STATE $sh_x,sh_y \sim \mathcal{U}(-\phi,\phi)$, $\theta \sim \mathcal{U}(-\psi,\psi)$ \STATE $y_{\text{pred}} \leftarrow \mathcal{F}\big[\mathcal{T}((x+\delta),(sh_x,sh_y,\theta))\big]$ \label{alg:eot} \STATE $\delta \leftarrow \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_o}{\partial \delta}$\COMMENT{$\rhd$ \blue{Update} } \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic} \label{algorithm:1}
elements.sent-2411-316: The implementation of our adversarial patch attack pipeline is detailed in Algorithm~\ref{algorithm:1 .}
elements.sent-2411-317: To improve the robustness of our attack under real-world scenarios, we employ random geometric transformations $\mathcal{T}\big[(\cdot),(sh_x,sh_y,\theta)\big]$ in line \ref{alg:eot with affine and rotation transformations~\cite{AthalyeEOT2018 as: \begin{equation} \mathcal{T}\big[(\cdot),(sh_x,sh_y,\theta)\big]= \begin{bmatrix} 1 sh_y sh_x 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} \cos \theta -\sin \theta \sin \theta \cos \theta \end{bmatrix}, \label{eq:trans}\end{equation} where $sh_x, sh_y \sim \mathcal{U (-\phi, \phi)$ are shear factors, and $\theta \sim \mathcal{U (-\psi, \psi)$ denotes the rotation angle, both sampled from uniform distributions during inner loop optimization step $k$.}}}}

elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Adversarial Patch Attack.} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE { Input:} $\mathcal{X}$: dataset; $\delta$: patch; $\mathcal{L}_o$: attack objective; $\mathcal{F}$: VLA model; $\mathcal{T}(\cdot)$: transformations; $\phi,\psi$: transformation parameters; T: attack steps; $k$: inner-loop steps. \STATE { Initialize:} $\delta \sim \mathcal{U}[0,1)$, $\mathcal{L}_o \in \{\mathcal{L}_{\text{UADA}}, \mathcal{L}_{\text{UPA}}, \mathcal{L}_{\text{TMA}}\} $ \FOR{$i = 1, 2, \cdots, T$} \FOR{$i = 1, 2, \cdots, k$} \label{alg:innerloop} \STATE $sh_x,sh_y \sim \mathcal{U}(-\phi,\phi)$, $\theta \sim \mathcal{U}(-\psi,\psi)$ \STATE $y_{\text{pred}} \leftarrow \mathcal{F}\big[\mathcal{T}((x+\delta),(sh_x,sh_y,\theta))\big]$ \label{alg:eot} \STATE $\delta \leftarrow \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_o}{\partial \delta}$\COMMENT{$\rhd$ \blue{Update} } \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic} \label{algorithm:1}

elements.sent-2411-405: Given the strong similarity between these patches and robotic arms in appearance, we hypothesize that these VLA-based models, in their current training paradigms, are often limited to a narrow range of robotic systems operating within restricted camera views.
elements.sent-2411-406: This constrained perspective camera pose may inadvertently induce a learned representation bias, where adversarial perturbations align with prominent structural features of the training robot's appearance in order to deceive current victim models.
elements.sent-2411-407: While VLA models have gained significant popularity due to their substantial robot capabilities, this study pioneers specific robotic attack objectives and demonstrates that these models are in fact vulnerable to various types of attacks.
